text:
Models have been improving at METR tasks, doing what would take humans longer and longer.
But have they been getting comparatively faster than humans at executing them?
There is a very smooth trend at the frontier of comparative speed, all the way back since GPT 3.5.
1/n https://t.co/wmYwZw5oXQ

raw.full_text:
Models have been improving at METR tasks, doing what would take humans longer and longer.
But have they been getting comparatively faster than humans at executing them?
There is a very smooth trend at the frontier of comparative speed, all the way back since GPT 3.5.
1/n https://t.co/wmYwZw5oXQ

text:
OpenAI dominated this frontier until the end of 2024, but Anthropic has been the leader since.
It is clear how much OpenAI has relied on scaling inference duration (not necessarily inference compute) to keep absolute performance at the frontier.
3/n

raw.full_text:
OpenAI dominated this frontier until the end of 2024, but Anthropic has been the leader since.
It is clear how much OpenAI has relied on scaling inference duration (not necessarily inference compute) to keep absolute performance at the frontier.
3/n

text:
Looking at the METR v1.1 task length per unit of execution time, we see a frontier with an exponential trend, from GPT3.5-Turbo to today.
For the P50 and P80 cases, we see doubling times of 282 and 346 days respectively.
2/n

raw.full_text:
Looking at the METR v1.1 task length per unit of execution time, we see a frontier with an exponential trend, from GPT3.5-Turbo to today.
For the P50 and P80 cases, we see doubling times of 282 and 346 days respectively.
2/n

text:
It is remarkable how smooth this metric's progresshas been. In part, fitting to only 5 points makes it easier to be smooth. But it still surprised me that it holds over paradigm changes, hardware generations, and the scaling of compute, data and investment over multiple OOMs.
4/4

raw.full_text:
It is remarkable how smooth this metric's progresshas been. In part, fitting to only 5 points makes it easier to be smooth. But it still surprised me that it holds over paradigm changes, hardware generations, and the scaling of compute, data and investment over multiple OOMs.
4/4

text:
It is no surprise that the doubling times of 282 and 346 days are much larger than the doubling times of the task length, given the explicit inference compute scaling (EICS).
But just like EICS kinked the METR task length plot, the efficiency plot can also be kinked.

raw.full_text:
It is no surprise that the doubling times of 282 and 346 days are much larger than the doubling times of the task length, given the explicit inference compute scaling (EICS).
But just like EICS kinked the METR task length plot, the efficiency plot can also be kinked.

text: Hardware that enables much higher throughput per user (e.g. Cerebras) or parallel generation (e.g. multi-agent frameworks) could lead to new laws at the frontier of task execution speed.

raw.full_text: Hardware that enables much higher throughput per user (e.g. Cerebras) or parallel generation (e.g. multi-agent frameworks) could lead to new laws at the frontier of task execution speed.

text: @IaimforGOAT Not sure if it helps that much, but I go super deep, not just a list of what was eaten. How ingredients are bought, how food is prepared and stored, what order it is eaten, what time of day, what things are eaten together.
